title: Chronic Pain and the Anger of Being Disbelieved
dek: *When doctors, family, and friends question whether your pain is real, the resulting anger doesn't disappear. It settles into your tissues, your jaw, your shoulders. This is about what happens when physical suffering meets social erasure, and why the rage matters as much as the pain itself.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Chronic pain patients face systemic disbelief that compounds physical suffering with invisible emotional trauma. The anger at being dismissed accumulates in the body, creating tension patterns that intensify pain and isolation. Recognition of this anger, and permission to feel it, becomes essential to both healing and reclaiming agency.

She sits in the rheumatologist's waiting room for the third time this year, rehearsing. Not rehearsing what hurts, she knows that cold, but rehearsing *how* to say it so they'll believe her this time. Her jaw aches from clenching. She doesn't notice. The receptionist calls her name and she stands, shoulders already apologizing, and walks toward another room where she'll need to prove, once again, that her body is telling the truth. ## The Invisible Wound of Not Being Believed Chronic pain occupies a strange territory in medicine. Unlike a broken bone visible on X-ray or a tumor outlined in a scan, many chronic pain conditions resist clean imaging. Fibromyalgia, complex regional pain syndrome, chronic fatigue syndrome, many forms of neuropathic pain, they exist in the lived experience of the sufferer more vividly than in the diagnostic tools meant to reveal them. This creates a peculiar kind of suffering. The pain itself is one layer. The disbelief, skepticism, or outright dismissal from medical professionals, family members, employers, even friends, that's another layer entirely. And sandwiched between them is something most people don't name: a deep, legitimate, corrosive anger. In my own practice, I've sat with people who apologize for being angry about their pain. They've internalized the message that anger is unproductive, that positivity heals, that they should be grateful for small mercies. Meanwhile their hands are fists under the table. Gabor Maté has written extensively about how emotional repression contributes to physical illness. He points out that when we chronically suppress feelings, particularly anger, the autonomic nervous system pays the price. The body doesn't distinguish between "appropriate" and "inappropriate" emotional expression. It only knows: expressed or unexpressed. Released or retained. When you're told your pain might be psychosomatic (with that particular tone that means "imaginary"), when a doctor spends ninety seconds with you before suggesting antidepressants, when your sister sighs and says "you're *always* tired," the anger you feel is not irrational. It's a healthy response to being erased. ## How Disbelief Lands in the Body Pat Ogden's work in sensorimotor psychotherapy reveals how psychological experience doesn't just affect the mind, it literally shapes the body's posture, tension patterns, and movement habits. When someone experiences chronic invalidation around their pain, the body responds. You might notice your shoulders riding up near your ears, a permanent brace against the next dismissal. Your jaw might clench so habitually you no longer feel it until someone points out the grinding sound at night. Your breath becomes shallow, confined to the upper chest, because deep breathing requires a softness you can't afford when you're perpetually defending your reality. These aren't metaphors. These are measurable, physical changes. Muscle tension increases pain. Shallow breathing reduces oxygen to tissues and triggers anxiety responses. The anger at being disbelieved quite literally compounds the pain you're already in. In our experience working with people in chronic pain, the moment they name the anger, really name it without apology or qualification, something shifts. Not the pain itself, necessarily, but the *relationship* to it. The pain stops being evidence of personal failure and becomes what it actually is: a physiological reality occurring in a body that deserves belief. ## The Social Isolation Loop Chronic pain already isolates. You cancel plans because you can't predict how you'll feel. You stop making plans because you're tired of canceling. You withdraw from activities that once defined you. The world of the healthy moves at a pace you can't match, and slowly, almost imperceptibly, you find yourself on the outside. Add disbelief to that isolation and it deepens into something darker. You stop talking about your pain because you're exhausted by the skepticism. You learn to perform wellness, to smile through it, to say "I'm fine" with the right inflection. The anger at having to perform, at being forced to minimize your reality for other people's comfort, it goes underground. Robert Augustus Masters writes about the difference between anger that is acknowledged and anger that is bypassed. Acknowledged anger can move through the system, be expressed appropriately, and resolve. Bypassed anger becomes resentment, bitterness, a chronic low-grade fury that seeps into everything. When you can't talk about your pain honestly, you can't talk about your anger at the disbelief honestly either. The isolation compounds. You're alone with the pain, alone with the disbelief, and alone with the rage at both. This is where many people discover [more articles in this journal](/articles) that speak directly to this experience. You're not alone in feeling this. The anger is shared. The pattern is recognizable. ## The Medical System's Gaslighting Problem Let's be specific about what happens in many clinical encounters. A patient, usually female, usually with a complex pain presentation, describes symptoms that don't fit a neat diagnostic box. The physician, pressed for time and trained in a model that privileges visible pathology, begins asking questions that subtly shift the frame. "How's your stress level?" "Are you sleeping well?" "Have you considered that anxiety might be strengthening this?" These aren't neutral questions. In context, they communicate: your pain might not be physical. It might be emotional. It might be, in the coded language of medicine, "all in your head." The patient, who has lived in this body for decades, who knows the difference between anxiety and nerve pain, between depression and inflammatory response, tries to redirect. But the script is already written. By the end of the appointment, she leaves with a prescription for an SSRI and a referral to a therapist, but no plan to address the pain itself. This is gaslighting. Not the hyperbolic internet version of the word, but the actual clinical definition: the systematic denial of someone's lived reality until they begin to doubt it themselves. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body acknowledges that chronic stress affects physical health, but the relationship isn't unidirectional. Chronic pain *causes* stress. Disbelief *causes* psychological distress. The arrow goes both ways. The anger at this systemic failure is not a symptom to manage. It's information. It's telling you that something unjust is happening, and your body knows it. ## What Happens to Unexpressed Rage Anger is energy. It's mobilizing, activating, designed to help you fight or set a boundary or say "no, this is not acceptable." When that energy has nowhere to go, when expressing it would be "inappropriate" or "difficult" or might jeopardize the care you desperately need, it doesn't dissolve. It stores. You might notice it in your neck, a chronic tightness that no amount of stretching releases. Or in your jaw, grinding teeth down to nubs. Or in your gut, the constant low-level nausea of swallowed rage. Or in your chest, a pressure that feels like grief but burns like fury. Some people turn it inward, into self-blame. "Maybe I am exaggerating. Maybe it's not that bad. Maybe I'm just weak." This internalized version is particularly toxic because it does the invalidator's work for them. You become your own skeptic. Others develop what looks like flatness, a kind of emotional numbing. When I ask "how do you feel about the doctor who dismissed you?" they say "I don't know, I guess I'm just used to it." But their hands are trembling. The body is holding what the conscious mind has learned not to feel. Over the years I've seen this pattern enough to recognize it instantly: the person who speaks very calmly about very enraging things, whose voice stays level while describing profound injustice. It's not peace. It's suppression. And suppression takes enormous energy, energy that could be directed toward actual healing. ## Permission to Be Angry (A Practice) Here's something you can do right now, wherever you are. It takes less than five minutes and requires nothing but privacy and honesty. Sit somewhere comfortable. Close your eyes if that feels right. Take three slow breaths, just to arrive in your body. Now say out loud, even if it's barely a whisper: "I am angry that my pain is not believed." Say it again. Notice what happens in your chest, your throat, your belly. Does the statement feel true? Does it feel dangerous to say? If it feels true, stay with it. Let yourself feel the anger for sixty seconds. Not the story about the anger, not the justification, just the raw sensation of it. Where does it live in your body? What's its temperature, its texture, its weight? Then say: "This anger is legitimate." Say it three times. Notice if your body resists this statement. Notice if a voice inside argues with it. You don't have to do anything with the anger. You don't have to express it to anyone or make a plan. This practice is just about recognition, about letting yourself feel what's true without immediately managing or minimizing it. When you're ready, open your eyes. The anger hasn't disappeared, and that's not the point. The point is you've acknowledged it exists, and acknowledgment is the first step toward letting it move. Some people find that herbs like [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) support the nervous system during periods of intense emotional processing, though this is always a complement to, not a replacement for, the deeper work of feeling. ## Reclaiming Your Story One of the most radical acts available to someone in chronic pain is to stop performing wellness for other people's comfort. To stop minimizing. To stop saying "I'm fine" when you're not. To stop protecting others from the reality of what you live with. This doesn't mean broadcasting your pain constantly or making it your identity. It means telling the truth when the truth is asked for. It means saying "actually, I'm in quite a bit of pain today" when someone asks how you are. It means not laughing along when someone makes a joke about people who are "always complaining" about their bodies. It means, sometimes, getting angry in front of people. Letting your voice rise. Saying "that's not acceptable" when a medical professional dismisses you. Setting a boundary even when it's uncomfortable. This is harder than it sounds. Many of us, particularly those socialized as women, learned early that our anger makes other people uncomfortable, and making others uncomfortable is the worst possible social crime. But your anger is not the problem. The disbelief that generated it is the problem. When you reclaim your story, you also reclaim your agency. You stop being a passive recipient of medical authority and become an active participant in your own care. You fire doctors who don't listen. You seek second, third, fourth opinions. You trust your own experience over someone else's skepticism. Resources like [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) on this site offer specific practices for working with anger in the body, for those times when you need concrete tools rather than abstract permission. ## The Link Between Anger and Advocacy There's a reason that patient advocacy groups are often founded by people who got angry enough to demand change. Anger, when it's not turned inward or suppressed, is clarifying. It shows you exactly what's unjust, and it provides the energy to confront that injustice. The disability rights movement wasn't built on patience and politeness. It was built on the collective rage of people who were tired of being invisible, tired of being dismissed, tired of a world that treated their bodies as problems to solve or ignore. Your personal anger about your personal experience of disbelief connects to a much larger pattern. You are not the first person to be told your pain is psychosomatic. You are not the first to have a doctor spend more time looking at their computer than at you. You are not the first to leave an appointment feeling smaller, doubted, erased. This doesn't make your experience less painful, but it does make it less isolating. The anger you feel is part of a collective anger, a justified fury at systems that consistently fail the people they're meant to serve. Some people channel this anger into activism, into changing protocols, into advocating for better training for medical professionals. Others channel it into peer support, into being the believing presence for others that they wished they'd had. Some simply use it to fuel their own persistent search for answers, refusing to accept dismissal as a final word. The work we do at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) often touches on this intersection between personal experience and collective pattern, exploring how individual healing connects to larger cultural shifts. ## When Anger Becomes a Doorway Here's what I've noticed about anger in chronic pain: when it's finally allowed, when it's really felt and not just intellectually acknowledged, it often reveals something underneath. Grief, maybe. Fear. A deep, aching loneliness. The anger was protecting these softer feelings, standing guard so you didn't have to feel how much it hurts to not be believed, how scary it is to live in a body that won't cooperate, how sad it is to be isolated from the life you thought you'd have. This doesn't mean the anger was "just" covering something else. The anger is real and legitimate on its own terms. But anger is often the emotion we can access when more vulnerable feelings are too threatening. In somatic work, there's a principle: follow the energy. If anger is what's alive in your system, start there. Feel it, track it, let it tell you what it needs to tell you. Don't rush to the "deeper" emotion. The anger itself is deep. But if you stay with it, if you let it move through rather than gripping it tight, you might notice it naturally shifts. The heat of rage might cool into the ache of grief. The defensive armor might soften into a simpler sadness. This is not a hierarchy, not anger being "less than" grief. It's just how emotional weather moves through a body when we stop managing it. For some people, supplements like [Source Naturals Serene Science 5-HTP, Balanced Mood 100 mg - 30 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0010ZWJMQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs Ashwagandha Root (180 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BRT9PQFC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can provide gentle nervous system support during intense emotional periods, though this varies widely by individual body chemistry. ## Building a Belief Network You can't control whether doctors believe you. You can't control whether your family understands. But you can, slowly and deliberately, build a small network of belief around yourself. This might mean finding one medical professional, even if it takes years, who listens without skepticism. It might mean connecting with online communities of people with your specific condition, where you don't have to explain or justify. It might mean working with a therapist who specializes in chronic illness and understands the psychological impact of disbelief. It definitely means believing yourself. On the days when everyone else's doubt feels louder than your own knowing, coming back to: I know what I feel. I know what's true in this body. I don't need external validation to trust my own experience. This is not the same as being certain about diagnosis or prognosis. You can hold uncertainty about what exactly is causing your pain while being absolutely certain that the pain is real. The two are not in conflict. Across the articles we've published on this site, this theme recurs: the necessity of self-trust in the face of systemic doubt. It's not a luxury. It's survival. Building this network also means being willing to remove yourself from relationships or situations that consistently invalidate you. This is hard when it's family. It's hard when it's a long-term doctor. But at some point, the cost of staying in invalidating relationships exceeds the cost of being alone. You have to protect your sanity, your sense of reality, your ability to trust your own body. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it normal to feel more angry about the disbelief than about the pain itself? Absolutely. Pain, especially when it's been chronic for a long time, can become almost familiar. You develop coping mechanisms, you adapt. But being invalidated, being told your reality isn't real, that hits a different nerve. It's a social and psychological injury layered on top of a physical one. Many people report that the anger at dismissal is actually more distressing than the pain itself, particularly because anger feels less "acceptable" than suffering does. You're not alone in this, and the anger is a sign your psyche is still fighting, still insisting on the truth. ### How do I express anger about medical dismissal without burning bridges with doctors I might need? This is a real dilemma, and there's no perfect answer. You can express dissatisfaction or disagreement firmly without aggression: "I don't feel heard in this appointment" or "I need you to take my pain report seriously" or "I'd like to explore other diagnostic options before accepting a psychological explanation." You can also simply leave. You can find another doctor. You don't owe anyone your continued patronage, even if they have MD after their name. Sometimes the bridge needs to burn. Sometimes protecting your dignity and your sense of reality matters more than maintaining access to a provider who isn't helping anyway. Trust your gut about which battles are worth fighting in the moment. ### Can anger actually make my pain worse? Suppressed anger, chronic tension, yes, that can absolutely intensify pain through muscle bracing, shallow breathing, and sustained stress hormone release. But the anger itself, the emotion, is not the problem. The problem is what we do with it. If you're clenching against it, pushing it down, maintaining a pleasant facade while internally screaming, that's what compounds pain. If you can find safe ways to express it, move it, release it, the anger itself won't hurt you. In fact, many people report that after really letting themselves feel and express anger, their pain temporarily lessens. The body relaxes when it's finally allowed to tell the truth. ## What Remains The anger doesn't resolve everything. Even when you name it, feel it, express it appropriately, set boundaries, find believing doctors, build your network of support, the pain itself might remain. Anger is not a cure. This isn't a story about how embracing your rage magically heals your body. But something else happens when you stop apologizing for your anger, when you stop performing wellness, when you insist on your own reality even in the face of doubt. You become more solid in yourself. Less fragile. Less dependent on external validation to know what's true. You still hurt. You're still limited by what your body can and cannot do. You still have to navigate a medical system that often fails people like you. But you're doing it as someone who trusts themselves, who knows their anger is legitimate, who refuses to disappear just because it would be more convenient for everyone else. That solidity, that self-trust, that refusal to vanish, it won't show up on any scan. No doctor will write it in your chart. But you'll feel it. And the people who love you well will see it. And slowly, imperceptibly, the world will have to make a little more room for the truth of your experience, because you're no longer willing to make yourself smaller to fit into their disbelief. The anger brought you here. Let it also carry you forward, into whatever comes next. You can find [the about page](/about) if you want to understand more about the work we do in this space, but mostly, just know: you're believed here. Your pain is real. Your anger is justified. And both deserve to be witnessed without apology. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on chronic pain and the anger of being disbelieved, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
